subject: general-knowledge
description: Cold War & Nuclear Era MCQs for PPSC, FPSC, NTS, CSS & PMS.

mcqs.q: Who coined the term 'Cold War'?
mcqs.explanation: The term 'Cold War' was coined by American financier and presidential advisor Bernard Baruch in a 1947 speech, describing the US-USSR rivalry.

mcqs.q: The Cold War strategy is best described as?
mcqs.explanation: The Cold War strategy was based on deterrence — the threat of massive nuclear retaliation to prevent either superpower from starting a war (MAD doctrine).

mcqs.q: Clausewitz's famous policy states that?
options: War is the continuation of policy
mcqs.explanation: Carl von Clausewitz, Prussian military theorist, famously stated 'War is the continuation of policy by other means' in his book 'On War'.

mcqs.q: The term 'Third World Countries' was first used during?
mcqs.explanation: 'Third World Countries' emerged during the Cold War to describe nations not aligned with either the US-led First World or Soviet-led Second World blocs.

mcqs.q: 'Third World Countries' means?
mcqs.explanation: Third World Countries generally refers to developing countries — those with lower economic development, primarily in Africa, Asia, and Latin America.

mcqs.q: In 2001, the World Trade Center attack took place in?
mcqs.explanation: The 9/11 World Trade Center attacks occurred in New York, USA on 11 September 2001, killing nearly 3,000 people.

mcqs.q: The site of the 9/11 attack was renamed?
mcqs.explanation: The former site of the Twin Towers in Manhattan was renamed Ground Zero after the September 11, 2001 attacks.

mcqs.q: Who gave the 'New World Order' speech at the time of USSR disintegration?
mcqs.explanation: President George H. W. Bush (Bush Senior) delivered his 'New World Order' speech on 11 September 1990, describing post-Cold War geopolitics.

mcqs.q: The US came to Pakistan as a frontline ally after which major event?
mcqs.explanation: US-Pakistan relations intensified after 9/11 (2001) when Pakistan became a frontline ally in the US War on Terror against the Taliban and al-Qaeda.

mcqs.q: Who was the US President during the Cuban Missile Crisis (October 1962)?
mcqs.explanation: President John F. Kennedy (JFK) led the US during the 13-day Cuban Missile Crisis of October 1962, confronting Soviet leader Nikita Khrushchev.

mcqs.q: Fidel Castro belonged to which country?
mcqs.explanation: Fidel Castro was the Cuban revolutionary and communist leader who ruled Cuba from 1959 to 2008.

mcqs.q: During the Cold War, Fidel Castro attempted to?
options: Allow a Soviet missile base in Cuba
mcqs.explanation: Fidel Castro allowed the Soviet Union to build nuclear missile bases in Cuba, triggering the Cuban Missile Crisis in October 1962.

mcqs.q: The term 'Facebook Diplomacy' was coined in?
mcqs.explanation: 'Facebook Diplomacy' was coined in December 2008 to describe diplomatic outreach via social media platforms.

mcqs.q: After North Korea's nuclear tests, how many countries are currently nuclear powers?
mcqs.explanation: There are 9 nuclear powers: USA, Russia, UK, France, China (NPT-recognized), plus India, Pakistan, North Korea, and Israel (undeclared).

mcqs.q: The IAEA (International Atomic Energy Agency) was recognized by the UN in?
mcqs.explanation: The IAEA was established in July 1957 and recognized by the United Nations as an autonomous international organization.

mcqs.q: The rule for international agreements in written form is provided in?
options: Vienna Convention on the Law of Treaties
mcqs.explanation: The Vienna Convention on the Law of Treaties (1969) regulates written international agreements between states.

mcqs.q: The Vienna Convention on the Law of Treaties is also known as?
mcqs.explanation: The Vienna Convention (1969) is called the 'Treaty of Treaties' because it governs how all other treaties are made, interpreted, and terminated.

mcqs.q: The NPT (1968) is about nuclear weapons and aims to?
mcqs.explanation: The Nuclear Non-Proliferation Treaty (NPT, 1968) aims to prevent the proliferation (spread) of nuclear weapons and promote peaceful nuclear energy use.

mcqs.q: Which country has threatened to withdraw from the NPT?
mcqs.explanation: Iran has repeatedly threatened to withdraw from the NPT (Nuclear Non-Proliferation Treaty) over Western sanctions and nuclear program disputes.

mcqs.q: The current Head of the IAEA (Director General) is?
mcqs.explanation: Rafael Mariano Grossi of Argentina has been Director General of the IAEA since 3 December 2019.

mcqs.q: The IAEA was created to promote?
mcqs.explanation: The IAEA was created under the 'Atoms for Peace' program to promote peaceful use of nuclear energy and prevent its diversion to military purposes.

mcqs.q: The IAEA has its headquarters in?
mcqs.explanation: The International Atomic Energy Agency (IAEA) is headquartered in Vienna, Austria at the UN Vienna International Centre.

mcqs.q: The world's largest nuclear power station is located in?
mcqs.explanation: The Kashiwazaki-Kariwa Nuclear Power Plant in Japan is the world's largest by installed capacity (7,965 MW across 7 reactors).

mcqs.q: North Korea conducted its second nuclear test in?
mcqs.explanation: North Korea conducted its second nuclear test on 25 May 2009, following its first test in October 2006.

mcqs.q: Which country built a cruise submarine threatening the US?
mcqs.explanation: North Korea announced the development of cruise-missile submarines, adding to concerns about its nuclear and military capabilities.

mcqs.q: The full form of 'CTBT' is?
mcqs.explanation: CTBT stands for Comprehensive Nuclear-Test-Ban Treaty, adopted in 1996 to ban all nuclear explosions for military or civilian purposes.

mcqs.q: The primary purpose of the AUKUS alliance is to?
options: Assist Australia in building nuclear submarines
mcqs.explanation: AUKUS (September 2021) between Australia, UK, and USA primarily aims to help Australia acquire nuclear-powered submarines to counter China.

mcqs.q: The Cold War was fought primarily between?
mcqs.explanation: The Cold War (1947-1991) was the ideological and geopolitical rivalry between the United States and the Soviet Union (USSR).

mcqs.q: The Cold War lasted from?
mcqs.explanation: The Cold War lasted from approximately 1947 (Truman Doctrine) to 1991 (dissolution of the Soviet Union).

mcqs.q: The 'Iron Curtain' speech was given by?
mcqs.explanation: Winston Churchill delivered the 'Iron Curtain' speech on 5 March 1946 at Fulton, Missouri, describing the Soviet division of Europe.

mcqs.q: The Truman Doctrine (1947) aimed to?
mcqs.explanation: The Truman Doctrine (1947) committed the US to containing Soviet communism, initially by providing aid to Greece and Turkey.

mcqs.q: The Marshall Plan (1948) provided aid to?
mcqs.explanation: The Marshall Plan (1948) provided $13 billion in US economic aid to rebuild Western Europe and prevent communist influence.

mcqs.q: NATO was formed in?
mcqs.explanation: The North Atlantic Treaty Organization (NATO) was established on 4 April 1949 via the Washington Treaty as a collective defense pact against the USSR.

mcqs.q: The Warsaw Pact was formed in?
mcqs.explanation: The Warsaw Pact was formed on 14 May 1955 as a Soviet-led military alliance of Eastern Bloc countries to counter NATO.

mcqs.q: The Korean War was fought from?
mcqs.explanation: The Korean War (1950-1953) was a Cold War conflict between North Korea (backed by USSR/China) and South Korea (backed by USA/UN).

mcqs.q: Korea was divided into North and South at which parallel?
mcqs.explanation: Korea was divided at the 38th parallel after WWII, formalized by the Korean War armistice of 1953.

mcqs.q: The Vietnam War was fought from?
mcqs.explanation: The Vietnam War (1955-1975) was a Cold War conflict ending with North Vietnam's victory and reunification under communist rule.

mcqs.q: The Berlin Wall was built in?
mcqs.explanation: The Berlin Wall was built by East Germany starting 13 August 1961 to prevent East Berliners from fleeing to West Berlin.

mcqs.q: The Berlin Wall fell in?
mcqs.explanation: The Berlin Wall fell on 9 November 1989, symbolizing the end of the Cold War and leading to German reunification in 1990.

mcqs.q: Mikhail Gorbachev introduced which reforms in 1985?
mcqs.explanation: Mikhail Gorbachev introduced Perestroika (restructuring) and Glasnost (openness) in 1985 to reform the Soviet system.

mcqs.q: The Soviet Union dissolved in?
mcqs.explanation: The Soviet Union was officially dissolved on 26 December 1991, ending the Cold War.

mcqs.q: The USSR disintegrated into how many independent states?
mcqs.explanation: The dissolution of the USSR in 1991 created 15 independent states including Russia, Ukraine, Belarus, Kazakhstan, and the Baltic states.

mcqs.q: The first atomic bomb was developed under which project?
mcqs.explanation: The Manhattan Project (1942-1945) was the US-led program that developed the first atomic bombs during WWII.

mcqs.q: The first atomic bomb was tested in?
mcqs.explanation: The world's first atomic bomb was detonated on 16 July 1945 at the Trinity Test site in New Mexico, USA.

mcqs.q: The USSR conducted its first nuclear test in?
mcqs.explanation: The Soviet Union conducted its first nuclear test (code name 'First Lightning' or 'Joe-1') on 29 August 1949.

mcqs.q: The UK conducted its first nuclear test in?
mcqs.explanation: The United Kingdom conducted its first nuclear test (Operation Hurricane) on 3 October 1952 at Monte Bello Islands, Australia.

mcqs.q: France conducted its first nuclear test in?
mcqs.explanation: France conducted its first nuclear test (Gerboise Bleue) on 13 February 1960 in the Algerian Sahara.

mcqs.q: China conducted its first nuclear test in?
mcqs.explanation: China conducted its first nuclear test on 16 October 1964 at the Lop Nur test site in Xinjiang.

mcqs.q: India conducted its first nuclear test in?
mcqs.explanation: India conducted its first nuclear test (Operation Smiling Buddha / Pokhran-I) on 18 May 1974.

mcqs.q: Pakistan conducted its first nuclear test in?
mcqs.explanation: Pakistan conducted its first nuclear tests (Chagai-I and Chagai-II) on 28 and 30 May 1998, becoming the 7th declared nuclear power.

mcqs.q: North Korea conducted its first nuclear test in?
mcqs.explanation: North Korea conducted its first nuclear test on 9 October 2006, making it the 8th declared nuclear power.

mcqs.q: The Non-Proliferation Treaty (NPT) entered into force in?
mcqs.explanation: The NPT was opened for signature in 1968 and entered into force on 5 March 1970.

mcqs.q: Which countries are NOT signatories to the NPT?
mcqs.explanation: India, Pakistan, Israel (undeclared), and North Korea (withdrew in 2003) are not members of the NPT.

mcqs.q: The CTBT was adopted by the UN General Assembly in?
mcqs.explanation: The Comprehensive Nuclear-Test-Ban Treaty (CTBT) was adopted by the UN General Assembly on 10 September 1996.

mcqs.q: SALT I (Strategic Arms Limitation Talks) was signed in?
mcqs.explanation: SALT I was signed on 26 May 1972 between the USA (Nixon) and USSR (Brezhnev), limiting strategic nuclear weapons.

mcqs.q: SALT II was signed in?
mcqs.explanation: SALT II was signed on 18 June 1979 between US President Carter and Soviet leader Brezhnev.

mcqs.q: The INF Treaty (1987) was signed between?
mcqs.explanation: The Intermediate-Range Nuclear Forces (INF) Treaty was signed in 1987 by Reagan and Gorbachev, banning land-based missiles of 500-5,500 km range.

mcqs.q: START I (Strategic Arms Reduction Treaty) was signed in?
mcqs.explanation: START I was signed on 31 July 1991 between US President George H. W. Bush and Soviet leader Gorbachev.

mcqs.q: Which US President had the 'Star Wars' (SDI) program?
mcqs.explanation: President Ronald Reagan announced the Strategic Defense Initiative (SDI), nicknamed 'Star Wars', on 23 March 1983.

mcqs.q: The Non-Aligned Movement was formally established in?
mcqs.explanation: The Non-Aligned Movement (NAM) was formally established at the Belgrade Conference in 1961. Its origins trace to the Bandung Conference of 1955.

mcqs.q: Who were the founding leaders of the Non-Aligned Movement?
mcqs.explanation: The Non-Aligned Movement was founded by Jawaharlal Nehru (India), Gamal Abdel Nasser (Egypt), Josip Broz Tito (Yugoslavia), Kwame Nkrumah (Ghana), and Sukarno (Indonesia).

mcqs.q: SEATO (Southeast Asia Treaty Organization) was formed in?
mcqs.explanation: SEATO was formed on 8 September 1954 as a US-led collective defense pact for Southeast Asia against communism.

mcqs.q: CENTO (Central Treaty Organization) was formed in?
mcqs.explanation: CENTO was formed in 1955 (originally as the Baghdad Pact) to counter Soviet expansion in the Middle East. Pakistan, Iran, Iraq, Turkey, and UK were members.

mcqs.q: The Soviet Union invaded Afghanistan in?
mcqs.explanation: The Soviet invasion of Afghanistan began on 24 December 1979, triggering a 10-year war that contributed to the USSR's collapse.

mcqs.q: The Soviet-Afghan War ended with the Geneva Accords in?
mcqs.explanation: The Soviet-Afghan War formally ended with the Geneva Accords signed on 14 April 1988, and Soviet troops withdrew by February 1989.

mcqs.q: Which summit marked the start of US-USSR détente?
mcqs.explanation: The Moscow Summit of 1972 between Nixon and Brezhnev, where SALT I was signed, marked the start of détente — easing of Cold War tensions.

mcqs.q: The Helsinki Accords were signed in?
mcqs.explanation: The Helsinki Final Act was signed on 1 August 1975 by 35 nations including the US, USSR, and European states, addressing European security and human rights.

mcqs.q: Who was the first leader to visit both USA and USSR during Cold War?
mcqs.explanation: Yugoslav leader Josip Broz Tito visited both superpowers to maintain his non-aligned status. Several non-aligned leaders followed suit.

mcqs.q: The Suez Crisis of 1956 involved?
mcqs.explanation: The Suez Crisis (October-November 1956) saw UK, France, and Israel invading Egypt after Nasser nationalized the Suez Canal.

mcqs.q: The Bay of Pigs invasion (1961) was an attempt to overthrow?
mcqs.explanation: The Bay of Pigs invasion (April 1961) was a failed CIA-backed attempt to overthrow Fidel Castro's communist government in Cuba.

mcqs.q: Which event marked the climax of Sino-Soviet split?
mcqs.explanation: The Sino-Soviet border conflict of 1969 on the Ussuri River marked the climax of the split between the USSR and China.

mcqs.q: U-2 Incident (1960) was when?
options: US spy plane shot down over USSR
mcqs.explanation: The U-2 Incident occurred on 1 May 1960 when USSR shot down a US U-2 spy plane piloted by Gary Powers, collapsing the Paris Summit.

mcqs.q: Mutually Assured Destruction (MAD) doctrine was based on?
mcqs.explanation: MAD doctrine held that massive nuclear retaliation would destroy both sides, preventing either superpower from launching a first strike.

mcqs.q: Who was the architect of US Cold War containment policy?
mcqs.explanation: Diplomat George F. Kennan authored the 1946 'Long Telegram' and 1947 'X' article outlining the containment strategy against Soviet expansion.

mcqs.q: The Nuclear Suppliers Group (NSG) was formed in response to?
mcqs.explanation: The Nuclear Suppliers Group (NSG) was formed in 1974 after India's first nuclear test to control the export of nuclear materials and technology.

mcqs.q: The 'Iron Curtain' divided?
options: Europe into Eastern and Western blocs
mcqs.explanation: The Iron Curtain was the political, military, and ideological barrier dividing Europe into the Soviet-dominated East and democratic West.

mcqs.q: Gorbachev won the Nobel Peace Prize in?
mcqs.explanation: Mikhail Gorbachev won the Nobel Peace Prize in 1990 for his role in ending the Cold War and opening up the Soviet system.

mcqs.q: The Cuban Missile Crisis was resolved on?
mcqs.explanation: The Cuban Missile Crisis was resolved on 28 October 1962 when Khrushchev agreed to remove Soviet missiles from Cuba in exchange for US concessions.

mcqs.q: The New START Treaty was signed in?
mcqs.explanation: New START (New Strategic Arms Reduction Treaty) was signed on 8 April 2010 by Obama and Medvedev, extended in 2021 through 2026.

mcqs.q: Who is considered 'Father of the Atomic Bomb'?
mcqs.explanation: J. Robert Oppenheimer, scientific director of the Manhattan Project, is known as the 'Father of the Atomic Bomb'.

mcqs.q: The Partial Test Ban Treaty (PTBT) was signed in?
mcqs.explanation: The Partial Nuclear Test Ban Treaty (PTBT) was signed on 5 August 1963 by USA, USSR, and UK, banning nuclear tests in atmosphere, space, and underwater.

mcqs.q: The Cuban Missile Crisis involved how many days?
mcqs.explanation: The Cuban Missile Crisis lasted 13 days, from 16 October to 28 October 1962.

mcqs.q: The Warsaw Pact was dissolved in?
mcqs.explanation: The Warsaw Pact was formally dissolved on 1 July 1991, following the collapse of communism in Eastern Europe.

mcqs.q: Russia is a founding member of which nuclear control body besides NPT?
mcqs.explanation: Russia is a founding and key member of the IAEA (since 1957) and was a central party to NPT, SALT, START, and INF treaties.

mcqs.q: Which treaty banned nuclear weapons in Latin America?
mcqs.explanation: The Treaty of Tlatelolco (1967) made Latin America and the Caribbean a nuclear-weapon-free zone.

mcqs.q: Which treaty made Africa a nuclear-weapon-free zone?
mcqs.explanation: The Treaty of Pelindaba (1996, in force 2009) established Africa as a nuclear-weapon-free zone.

mcqs.q: The Outer Space Treaty (1967) bans?
mcqs.explanation: The Outer Space Treaty (1967) bans placing nuclear weapons and weapons of mass destruction in orbit or on celestial bodies.

mcqs.q: The Chernobyl nuclear disaster occurred in?
mcqs.explanation: The Chernobyl nuclear disaster occurred on 26 April 1986 in Ukrainian SSR — the worst civilian nuclear accident in history.

mcqs.q: The Fukushima nuclear disaster occurred in?
mcqs.explanation: The Fukushima Daiichi nuclear disaster occurred on 11 March 2011 in Japan, triggered by a magnitude 9.0 earthquake and tsunami.

mcqs.q: The Treaty on the Prohibition of Nuclear Weapons (TPNW) entered into force in?
mcqs.explanation: The TPNW (Nuclear Ban Treaty) was adopted in 2017 and entered into force on 22 January 2021. No nuclear-armed state is party to it.

mcqs.q: 'Facebook Diplomacy' is a term for?
options: Use of social media in diplomacy
mcqs.explanation: 'Facebook Diplomacy' refers to the use of social media platforms like Facebook by governments and diplomats to conduct outreach and public diplomacy.

mcqs.q: In 2019, the US deputy defense secretary warned which country about nuclear testing?
mcqs.explanation: In 2019, the US deputy defense secretary warned Syria about nuclear weapons testing and development programs.

mcqs.q: Which phrase describes the period of nuclear arms buildup by superpowers?
mcqs.explanation: The Cold War 'nuclear arms race' saw the US and USSR stockpile tens of thousands of nuclear warheads, peaking in the 1980s.

mcqs.q: The term 'domino theory' during Cold War claimed?
options: One country falling to communism would trigger others
mcqs.explanation: The 'domino theory' during Cold War held that if one Southeast Asian country fell to communism, neighboring countries would follow.

mcqs.q: The Soviet Union's Afghanistan invasion happened in the tenure of which US President?
mcqs.explanation: The Soviet invasion of Afghanistan (December 1979) occurred during US President Jimmy Carter's tenure. Carter cut SALT II ratification in response.

mcqs.q: The 'Reagan Doctrine' aimed to?
mcqs.explanation: The Reagan Doctrine (1980s) supported anti-communist resistance movements worldwide, including mujahideen in Afghanistan and Contras in Nicaragua.
